steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks which objection Text 2's author would most want to rule out regarding Text 1's finding. Text 1 claims entrepreneurial training caused participants to show high likelihood of starting new businesses. Text 2 criticizes such studies for three flaws: insufficient sample sizes, lack of control groups, and no pretraining baselines. The relevant flaw here is the lack of control groups — without a comparison group that received no training, you cannot show the training itself caused the outcome.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — The participants would have responded to the training differently if they had not known that they were participating in a study.: this is observer bias (demand characteristics), which Text 2 does not mention as a concern. ✗
  • Choice B — The participants would have shown high likelihood of creating new businesses regardless of whether they received the training.: this is exactly the objection a missing control group fails to rule out — maybe these participants were already entrepreneurially inclined before the study. ✓
  • Choice C — The participants would have been less likely to show high likelihood of creating new businesses if the study sample size had been smaller.: sample size affects statistical power, not whether the training caused the result; this conflates two separate issues. ✗
  • Choice D — The participants would have shown greater responsiveness to the training if the training sessions had lasted longer.: training duration is not among Text 2's stated shortcomings. ✗
That is the correct answer.
